sentences.text: And the  one that I remember, that goes back to the ghetto.
You want to know about it, what I remember?
It came in because, at once, you know, talking and talking, I missed [INAUDIBLE].
It was in the beginning when the ghetto was established, they were not so strict, because, still, they didn't, the Gestapo didn't touch us.
We were-- And they want everybody to get, first, into the ghetto.
And-- but the Judenrat selected how many people should go out daily to work, to the-- to [NON-ENGLISH] where the German Army was.
Not only the Army, there was Gestapo SS.
And I forgot about them.
There were young people in the black uniforms.
I forgot how they called them, with the, a, on their heads they had-- I don't remember.
A special mark on their head, they had.
They were the young.
These were the worst.
I forgot how they called them.
So these were there, but they didn't touch us at the begin.
Though when people went out to work, we could go out, but they said, if someone's left in the ghetto, old people out of work, someone's left, even women and children, they will take them away.
Though we wanted to go out.
So we took, I took my little daughter and my sister took her little son-- he was a few years older-- and we went together with the people to work there.
We didn't work there, but there, we wanted to hide somewhere.
That was a big park.

You couldn't find a place in the park, but my sister went with her husband, with my brother-in-law over-- he was assigned to the, as I said before, to the Italian Division-- and I was left with my little daughter.
And I looked around, what to do, where to hide here?
I've seen a patch of cabbage.
No one will believe it, but it was quite high, the cabbage, big heads of cabbage in rows.
And between them, there was a little space.
I have seen some SS approaching.
They haven't seen me, but I've seen them from a distance, from their buildings going out.
And quickly, I laid down in, on the patch of cabbages.
There was still room enough on my front, I put my daughter before me.
I laid her down and I said, shh-shh.
She understood.
As little as she was, she understood.
And she was flat laid down on the patch between the cabbages, they were quite high, and they passed and didn't see us.
They passed just near it.
Further, I knew that they would go out this way.
So they-- they haven't noticed.
They-- they wouldn't think of someone hiding between the cabbages.
They passed, and after they left, I didn't want, I thought maybe they would come back and see us.
I got up and no one was there.
The soldiers were quite distant from us.
And I notice a little building, like a house, a little, a small house, and that was a caretaker's.
The patch of cabbages and other, he had just a small garden, what he needed for himself.
So I went to that house and he was there.
He came out.
And he was like a caretaker there.
And I said, please, help me.
I'm with my child.
I was in-- I told him the story between the cabbages, and if they go back, they will see me.
I don't know where to go.
All right.
He took me in.
He helped us.
He helped, later, my sister, too, with her little boy.
She went out and couldn't go back, so he took her in, too.
Because they never questioned him.
They knew he lived there.
It-- In the whole little house were two rooms, but the windows were very low.
If we stood inside and looked out the windows, we could see not far.
We could see-- it was a very low one-- the boots of the soldiers, of the Germans as they passed.
They went around.
They never went in, they never ask him.
Because they couldn't think of anything, a Jewess with a child could hide here before their noses.
And he kept me there, day and night, for about three days inside.
He said, I'm afraid.
Maybe they'll want to come in.
But they never come in, as they had nothing to do there.
They knew he lives there, a man, caretaker.
He is tending his little garden there, and he, when they need him, they called him out if he had to do something inside, in there.
A garden is on, a one-- they had a few buildings around, quite more than a few.
And that how it was three days.
And then he said, I'm sorry.
I can't keep you longer, I'm afraid.
He even fed me, brought a piece of bread for me and for my child.
And--
--he was a Pole.
No.
No.
I never ask and I wasn't interested then.
I was interested only to hide somewhere.
And I could see through the window, you could see them going in, 'cause they would never go in there.
And later, as I've seen the people, our people, the  Jewish people forming, you know, a group to go from back home late in the afternoon, I joined and I went back to the ghetto.
That was in the beginning.
And later, what happened to my daughter and how I got out of, I told you later.
I said that later, how I got out of the ghetto.
And that was in '40, beginning '42.
They were still there, but I remember when-- That's what I want to paint, if I have time.
It would take a long time, what I have seen.
When they killed the, all the children they took out from the ghetto.
That was in '42, too, yes.
Well, I told you I have seen a truck with children.
Yeah, I don't know exactly when.
I think it was beginning '42.
Yes, yes.
Two weeks, only.
Yes, yes.
Pardon?
I think it was in winter time.
It was in the winter in '42, beginning of middle somewhere.
Yes, because it was snowing.
Because I found my daughter--
--was sleeping there, and she was covered in snow.
So of course it was cold.
That was when I was [INAUDIBLE] and when my daughter was in the hospital.
And I was, visit Polish woman who had two children.
But during the day, I-- I had no food, and it was very cold in there.
Her, she had only one room, that was all, and not even a floor, a wooden floor, nothing for the-- only the earth.
And I had to go daily to the hospital to see what happens to my daughter there when I left her there, and if she's getting better.
I hoped so.
And once-- and she knew that I go out.
I don't think that she even locked-- didn't have a key-- locked her little place where she lives, that woman.
She wasn't rich, she had nothing inside.
An old wooden bed and some rags, that's all what she had there.
She was very-- very poor.
And the-- so I went out.
I had to go to the hospital.
I don't know was it that I had so much courage.
I wasn't afraid to walk on the streets, and not like other people had, false papers.
As I mentioned before, I had only a birth certificate which I kept in my pocket.
I didn't show it to anybody.
And I, and I even didn't remember that I have it there.
I just walked on the streets not being afraid, not even knowing that I'm hungry.
I didn't feel hunger.
I think as longer you go without food, you get used to it.
The-- I think that the, well, it conditions you.
I wasn't hungry, not thirsty.
I just went to the street and wanted to go, and it was a long, long time to go to the hospital.
I-- I don't know how many kilometers-- it was kilometers-- to walk to the hospital.
And they didn't expect Jews to walk on the streets.
The ghetto was still there, but shortly before they took them all out or killed them.
They must've been still-- I don't know.
I was not in contact anymore with the ghetto.
And once as I walked down, it was [?
Grudetska ?] Street, walked down the street, I've seen a-- a-- a big truck, one truck.
I just had to-- to think hard, what is it what I see?
I couldn't believe it.
There were, in front in helmets, before the truck, two Germans.
Don't know what they-- what ranks they had, in their shiny boots with helmets, armed, of course, two on the front, two on the side, and two at the back.
And I thought of myself, whom are they guarding so-- They just guarded if someone would run away from this truck.

And there were-- It was the middle of winter, very cold, and there were naked children, you could see-- That's what I want from memory to paint, if I can do it-- you could see frozen, and hear screams.
Because underneath it, all the children, they took out.
I can't say exactly how many there were.
There must have been about, in a big truck, about near 100, 80 or 90 children.
Underneath, they were warm still, because the top was frozen, covered some of the children on top.
Underneath, they screamed.
It must be terrible.
Just-- one can't think of it, what deaths they had, alive all.
And you could see only stiff arms and legs, just-- they were one over the other, a heap, just quite a big heap of children, one over-- they threw them out, not thinking.
And some heads felling over on the edge, and other stiff, frozen necks hanging down, and arms on top.
I just looked at it and I didn't know what I'm seeing.
I couldn't believe it.
But I felt faint, because I was on the path on one side, and there was-- on the other side in the center was that truck moving slowly.
And I just felt-- I said, no.
If you faint here-- so the police was everywhere around-- they will know that you are Jewish, so you can't faint.
Don't faint.
But I feel that my knees got soft, that I'm going to fall.
I look out on my right, just near, not far, was a shop, two steps to go up.
But there are no Jewish shops.
They were all Poles or Ukrainians.
But I couldn't do anything else and I, not thinking, I went in then up.
It was hard to get up the two steps.
I went in.
There were people.
No one, no one was interested.
People went to the street as [BACKGROUND NOISE] that bus with children, not a bus, a truck, open, big truck.
There was some wood or coal.
That's how they thought of it.
No one understood.
No one was interested.
No one looked, even.
I was the only one, I think, that I've seen it.
And they went after their business.
So inside were some people, too, and no one noticed me, and I went in.
But the woman, the proprietor had seen me.
Could be she was a good-hearted person, because she left and went in, and got a glass of water for me, and gave me a chair to sit down.
So, so quietly, she didn't ask anything.
So she-- she didn't want others to notice.
And after I sat for a few minutes, I was afraid to sit there and drink the water.
I got up, the truck was gone.
And so, as I felt like this, I had to go kilometers to my, to see my daughter in the hospital.
So I thanked her.
I couldn't even say a word, just, I nod my head.
I nod to her and went out and came to the hospital.
And no one had seen me in the street that passed by, no one looked at me.
I think if one is very confident and courageous, one can do many things.
With this--
And so, I reached the hospital.
And as usually-- it happened before-- they said, we don't know.
I said, is she getting better?
No.

The sisters [INAUDIBLE] replaced the Polish ones for Ukrainians, and she said, no, they didn't know they were other sisters, not the one who took me in when I fainted, when I fell, when I came in the first time.
I just asked in Polish, is she better?
So she said, no.
They were religious.
She said, [NON-ENGLISH].
It mean, one God knows what will happen.
So with a heavy heart, I had to leave and go back the same way, and go back to the Polish, to the woman where I stayed.
And so it, it happened.
It was about three weeks when once I came, and I said I said it before, that I asked, I want to see her.
Because it was infectious, they didn't let in anyone.
Only through the window, from outside, you could see inside.
They took the child to the window and I was outside.
And I waited and waited, and I haven't seen my child.
As I was waiting, there were mothers, happy ones, Poles, laughing to the children, children laughing, smiling.
And they all left and I still waited.
They brought, as I said, the child to the window.
And finally, I had a look, and the sister knocked on the window to me, and showed me, then said, that's your child.
I said, no.
No.
I couldn't recognize her.
Only three weeks, it was not the same.
The hair all cut off.
She looked to me like a little boy.
The face, a quarter of her face, she was so thin, because they didn't feed them there.
They didn't have-- the Germans didn't give anything.
Even to the Ukrainians, they didn't give to the hospital anything, no food delivered.
I don't know what they had said, how they lived there, and many children died at the hospital.
And as I looked further-- I didn't want to go away-- I felt I must see my child.
And then I had another look and she smiled.
She-- I didn't recognize her, but she smiled.
It wasn't a smile.
It was just thin white line here, but I noticed that she smiled to me.
She recognized me, because I was the same, but she was not the same.
I do anything you want, please.
Do it for me.
I promised her that I will be her sister after the war.
Maybe it will be different.
I will help her if I-- if it will be possible.
And somehow, I convinced her and she went.
And a few hours later, she brought her home, brought her to me.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't have anything.
I couldn't give her-- she was still not well.
She hardly could speak.
She needed different care as a sick child.
But in those circumstances, I couldn't do anything.
I hugged her.
And as I stood there and thought what to do, although I couldn't do anything.
She said to me, I can't keep you both here, or-- you or you're child.

And as you have been-- because before, I've been in prison, [NON-ENGLISH], as you have been in prison, those-- the police who went after you, they will recognize you, and they will-- if they see you, you'll lead them to my place.
They will go after you and come in here, and see your child here, and start questioning, whose child is it?
And you, yourself, because you told-- I told them that I'm not from here.
I'm from Brody.
I want to go back.
So she said the best things for the people, you go-- I'll keep the child and you go out, and says outside on the street.
Said, just gather people to be sent to Germany for forced labor.
So she said, you, once you'll be outside on the street and nowhere else to go, I'm sure that they will take you.
Just-- you have to do it and send you to Germany.
Because they-- if people were unemployed on the streets, they surrounded them and send them to Germany, forced labor.
So all the same, I didn't know where, why-- I know, of course, Germany [BACKGROUND NOISE] and what will happen there, and if not for a certain death.
But she didn't want to keep me anymore.
So I left the child, without a word, without saying anything.
She didn't let me even go near to her.
Said, just go out, quickly go out.
So I went.
That was in '42.
Yes.
Could be, because it was already warm and it wasn't so cold.
And when I delivered her to the hospital, it was cold, winter.
And that could be it was springtime.
I don't know.
I never thought of an Easter there.
So that's why they took me later, as I said, to that place where they gathered people.
They had to have such a-- a number of people to send to Germany.
They were frozen.
The ones underneath, because it was warm, the top covered them.
I don't think that anyone told that story, any survivor, because no one had see.
I was only-- perhaps there were other Jews like myself on the streets, but not at that particular place.
I went there because I was, the-- the woman, the Polish woman lived not far from there, and that's when I went out on my way to the hospital.
No.
No.
They-- the truck went on-- the-- the station that was called [?
Rudetska ?] is a street.
It was a long street that led to this station.
There was only one station in Lwow, a big station.
And further was a huge place where they-- I remember it was a coal-- you know coal that people used to burn in their stoves?
Poland had a lot of coal, like coal mines, something like it.
And there was a special-- after that station, it was still the same station, but further, much further, a huge open place.
And I remember that I have seen it once.
They filled in the-- the trains or wagons with coal.
From place to place, they had to send it over, the other parts of Poland.
But you know, they-- later, someone told me, I've heard, that over there, because it's a open space, they took them there.
And I don't know if they-- they just threw them out of the truck, if they shot the ones who were still alive.
I don't know if hardly was any of them.
But that, I remember.
That was all the children from the, from the ghetto.
I haven't heard this story before.
But I have seen it, because I walked on the streets.
Yes.
One think only.
I remember as I walked on the street, there must be other people that the Germans didn't recognize.
They didn't, they didn't think of Jewish people walking the streets.
There was a man.
Man were on false papers, too, if they survived.
I don't believe it, but I've seen them out of the ghetto, maybe.
Because as I passed the street, you see, Germans didn't, couldn't know who I am.
But a Jew could always recognize another Jew, even if I didn't look Jewish, as they say.
Because I passed by, and a man-- he was fair, blond, too-- and he's-- as I passed by, we didn't stop to talk.
I didn't know him.
He just said, [NON-ENGLISH], and I've heard that.
[NON-ENGLISH] is from [INAUDIBLE] Israel.
[INAUDIBLE] the-- the Polish [NON-ENGLISH], Polski, [?
Narujadowski, ?] [?
Israelski. ?] It's from [INAUDIBLE] Israel, [NON-ENGLISH], shortened [NON-ENGLISH].
I didn't know what it means then.
But as I passed by, he said [NON-ENGLISH].
After the war, I ask someone.
He said, yes.
That's how a Jew if he's, he didn't stop but he-- he-- he-- he knew that I'm Jewish.
Why?
I don't know.
If he knew me before or not, I have no idea.
But he passed by and he said-- just wanted to know, to be sure, and then he said, quietly said, [NON-ENGLISH]?
I didn't answer, because I didn't know that word.
Later I found out.
Thank you.
